## [00:00 - 03:00]

Okay, so this video is all about what I call the PLM model, the product launch manager model. Now, I'm gonna go deep here. This is the model where you actually get, I mean, you basically become part of the team and you are the one running the launch. Once you have one success doing this, then you're not gonna be hurting for clients. Getting the first client might be a little harder, but just the word of mouth from being a product launch manager can be very, very strong. And I will tell you that I regularly get people coming to me asking for a referral to a product launch manager. There are more positions open than there are launch managers. Then there are qualified launch managers. So, this can work really, really well. I'm not saying it's easy. And client selection is absolutely paramount. So, one question, and this applies with the bigger launches. As you get into a more developed business, especially with partners, is team. Who's going to bring the team? Is your client going to bring the team? Or are you gonna bring the team? And actually, over the years, there have been people that have gone deep in this PLM business, and they have developed an entire launch team that they would bring in. But then when you do that, you're talking about integrating teams. For a larger business with a larger launch, then a big launch doesn't happen without a team. And there's someone on my client's team was actually editing my emails. They were changing things in my emails. ## [03:00 - 06:00]

of how you're gonna work with their team, how they're gonna work with you, one super important piece is you have to have authority in this relationship. You can't have your client second-guessing you. You can't have your client's team second-guessing you. So everything you do in working together, you have to go in where they understand you are the launch expert, and you know what you're talking about. And it's not like you're a dictator, because there has to be a flow of ideas and information back and forth, but at the end of the day, they have to know that you know what you're doing. You can't have them second-guessing pieces of, once you put together a strategy, you follow through on it. Once you put together a copy, you follow through. Once you put together a script, they follow through that. They're not second-guessing you. So part of this is, as I mentioned in one of the earlier videos, is you have to get paid up front, and you have to get paid a retainer. And part of that is protecting you, because you've taken this time, and you're putting all this time and effort before you get your percentage of the launch, but another part of it is when they're paying you a lot of money, then they are going to listen to you more readily. You've got more authority. So that getting paid up front has got multiple purposes. One of them is so that you're getting paid for all that work, and you're not waiting, and waiting, and waiting. Another is so that you have that authority. Because if you don't have that authority, they're not gonna listen to you. If they're gonna listen to you, but then do something differently, they're gonna shoot themselves in the foot, your launch will underperform, they'll make less money, they'll be less happy, and it's just bad all around. This is, if client selection is the number one thing in this business, number two is maintaining your authority, establishing and maintaining your authority. You gotta get that straight. Okay, so your number one job here, your number one job is generally going to be pushing your client. You're gonna be pushing them to make a great offer. You know, at the end of the day, the launch process is amazing, but unless you have a great offer, it's not gonna convert. And a great offer is usually requires a lot more work. You know, anyone could throw a slap to something together and put out a half-assed offer that's okay, that's somewhat interesting. Well, whatever they bring to you, like this is what we're gonna put out into the world, 95% of the time, you're gonna say, that's great. And can we add this, and this, and this, and this, and this? And you're gonna have a lot of ideas based on your market research for what would make an even more compelling offer. And they're gonna push back because they're not gonna wanna do all the extra work that you're gonna ask them to do.

## [06:00 - 09:03]

But this is gonna be one of the biggest things you do is coach them and consult with them and control them and tease them and beat them into creating an absolutely irresistible offer. That's your job. Your job is just to make them create something great, to create a great, super compelling offer that's gonna make a huge impact on whoever you sell to. And then you're gonna have to hold their feet to the fire so they not only agree to do it, but they actually create it. That, you know, I mean, it's like, this is your coaching mode, you know, where you shift into coaching mode. Because it's like, I mean, there's every great athlete has got a coach. Every great athlete's got a coach. And what the coach does is they point out things that the athlete can't see themselves. But they also push them. They push them to do better because they can see the greatness within them. And that's what you have to do. You have to see the greatness within your client and push them to create an amazing offer. Then you have to push them to actually ship it, to actually get it done, to put a date on the calendar and say, we are gonna hit this date and then have them hit it. And you know, sometimes, you know, there's been many launches where dates slept and moved out and sometimes it's inevitable. But you have to push them to actually get it done, to actually ship it, to create the product. There's, in this business, there are so many perfectionists and you very likely will have a perfectionist that you're working with. And you have to push them to get past that, just to get the product out there. Because at the end of the day, until the product gets out there, it's not gonna help anyone. And oh, by the way, it's not gonna make any money. You know, a very, very good product that ships is a million times better than the perfect product that never ships. So you gotta push them to create a great offer, push them to create the product. Then, you're gonna have to push them to do the pre-launch content. Probably not that big a deal, but then you have to push them to sell it. You have to push them to sell with conviction, to get in front of that camera and understand they don't have to be slick. They don't have to be professional. They don't have to be slimy. But they have to make a compelling offer. They have to focus on the impact they're gonna make, the good they're gonna do, how they're gonna help their clients and have them sell with conviction. And if they shoot that sales video and it's just not quite there, you gotta get them to do it again. And maybe again, they have to sell with conviction. You need a client that can sit there and deliver on the real value, the value proposition, how they're gonna help and then ask for the order.

## [09:03 - 12:04]

So, like I said, your number one job here is pushing people, push them. Okay, so there's three primary roles you're going to have, big picture. And it's really clear, it's important you get clear on these roles. And as a product launch manager, you might or might not have responsibility for all these, but there's three primary roles in any launch. And one of them is just the pure logistics of like how it's actually gonna work, what shopping cart are you gonna use, what mail host you're gonna use, how you're gonna manage, if there's a team, how you're gonna manage the team, what project management software will you use, how you communicate with each other, how you're gonna shoot videos, who's gonna shoot them, where they're gonna get hosted. There's lots and lots of details. How are pages gonna get built, what shopping cart are you gonna use, what merchant account are you gonna use, how are you gonna handle customer service, what help desk are you gonna need, lots and lots of details. And they can be, you can do them super complex, or you can do them super simple. I've done the whole spectrum. I've run a launch literally out of a Gmail box and just the simplest pages in the world, or I've gone super complicated. Whole big spectrum, and where you land on that spectrum is gonna depend on a thousand different factors, but that's one piece in managing a launch. Not the most thrilling, most exciting part, not the part that frankly brings the most dollar value to the launch, but that's one part. And as a product launch manager, you might or might not have responsibility for that, this is all part of the negotiations. Another part is the actual launch strategy. And this is the part that was sort of, in my wheelhouse, the part that I tended to do, and this is what's our overall theme for the launch? What's the hook for the launch? What's the offer? What's the offer going to be called? What are the bonuses gonna be? What is the pre-launch content look like? What's the overall story arc? What's that first PLC one going to be? What's PLC two gonna be? What's PLC three gonna be? How are they gonna tie together? How are you gonna ask for comments? What is the sales video, the sales letter? Is there a sales video or a sales letter or both? What goes in there? What's the open? What's the hook? What's the headline? What's the pricing? How do you reveal price? Where do you put in case studies? Is there a webinar part of this? Is there a live stream part of this? What's, where does the scarcity come in? What's the open cart plan? How many days do you leave the cart open? What do you mail on day two, three, and four of open cart? Hot on and on and on, all the strategy, the marketing strategy. So that's a really big piece. And that's huge high value piece there.

## [12:04 - 15:05]

That's really my, you know, one of my genius zones. And that's when I was a product launch manager, the piece that I would bring to the table. So first, logistics, second is actual launch strategy. And the third one is JVs and JV strategy. And not every launch is a JV launch or a joint venture launch. Or, you know, not every launch uses affiliates. But if yours does, then this is a major piece. And there's the recruitment, the attraction, the recruitment, getting them stoked, getting them excited. And then there's the nitty gritty management of like, they mail you and they say, I need a link. I don't know what my link is. I lost the password. Can I mail PLC one after PLC two is really, just nitty gritty stuff, right? And so those are the three primary pieces. And you have to be clear with yourself and also with your client, which of those three you're gonna bring. So typically I would bring the launch strategy and then the JV management. Now, when you get into one of these deals, if you're planning on doing a JV launch, a lot of times there's a whole question of who's gonna bring the JVs? Are you gonna bring the JVs, the affiliates, or is the person you're working with gonna bring them? A lot of times they're like, well, I traded emails with so-and-so one time, so they're gonna be part of it. And this is a piece of client selection. I forgot to mention. If you're gonna do a JV launch, if they're promising that they've got JVs, if they've got affiliates already, you gotta be really careful, because a lot of times people think they've got potential partners, and really that's just someone they met once, or someone said, oh yeah, I'll put something together, I'll mail for you. And it's a whole world of difference. It's someone saying, yeah, I've got something to mail for you, and them actually going to bat and really knowing how to support a launch and really going all out in a launch. So those are little things you have to look at and judge, little things that will make a huge difference. So if you're going to be the person running the JVs, there's the whole management piece, the nitty gritty piece, but then there's the sort of the JV stoke, getting them stoked, getting them excited, getting them amped up, and really it's a second launch. The launch to get your partners excited and remote for you, and that mail just once, but mail for each piece of pre-launch, and then mail multiple times during cart, there's a whole art form involved there. And that's usually where a launch manager can bring a lot of value there, but it's a big piece, it's a huge piece. ## [15:05 - 16:47]

but I got to the point where if I got involved as a launch manager, then that would, a lot of people would come into that launch just because they knew I was a launch manager, and they knew the launch was gonna come off really, really well. And so then, it's sort of like, it's a feeding frenzy. It's just a feeding frenzy. If you solve a sudden, there's like, wow, Jeff's involved with this launch, he thinks it's gonna go good, it's gonna go good, so we're gonna promote. And it becomes a self-perpetuating cycle. If you can get into that type of position where you've had success as a launch manager, and you've brought success to JVs, and now they know that your next launch that you do is gonna be good because you're involved, then it's an absolute win. So product launch manager, the PLM model, you get paid a percentage of the launch, absolutely great model if you get the right client, who, and you've got the authority to really drive them, to create a great offer, to create a great pre-launch, and to bring it in on time. And then once you do it once, then you're going to have more people that wanna work with you, then you've got time to work with. So go for it. It's a great way to get started. It requires work, it requires commitment, and it requires having the spine to negotiate a great, first of all, pick the great partner, the great person you're gonna work with, and then have the spine to actually negotiate a great deal and stick to that great deal. So go get them.
